target.field_a: Cryo-EM single-particle analysis and heterogeneity methods
target.field_c: Bacterial outer membrane vesicle (OMV) cargo sorting mechanism

checks.check: OMV size vs cryo-EM resolution range
checks.details: OMVs: 20-250 nm diameter. Cryo-EM single-particle resolution: 2-4 Angstrom for proteins > 50 kDa. Subtomogram averaging: 10-30 Angstrom for in situ structures. OMV diameter is well within cryo-EM/cryo-ET imaging range. Individual cargo proteins on OMV surface (OmpA, OmpC, etc.) at 35-40 kDa are at the lower limit for single-particle analysis but can be resolved in the context of the OMV membrane. Subtomogram averaging of budding sites is feasible at ~2 nm resolution.

Standard OMV purification (ultracentrifugation, density gradient) yields 10^10-10^12 particles/L culture. Cryo-EM single-particle analysis requires ~10^6-10^8 particles per grid. OMV yield is sufficient for cryo-EM sample preparation. SEC-based purification provides cleaner samples with less flagella/pili contamination.

CryoDRGN requires consistent particle alignment. OMVs are variable in size and composition, which is precisely the heterogeneity to be analyzed. However, cryoDRGN was designed for conformational heterogeneity of a SINGLE protein complex, not for compositional heterogeneity of a heterogeneous membrane-bound compartment. For 3D classification of OMV populations by size/morphology, standard RELION 3D classification is more appropriate than cryoDRGN. CryoDRGN would be appropriate for analyzing conformational states of specific OMV-associated protein complexes (e.g., T6SS components).

In situ cryo-ET of bacterial cells with subtomogram averaging of membrane budding sites is well-established (Oikonomou & Jensen 2017 for T6SS, Yao et al. 2012 for flagellar motor). OMV budding sites are rarer and less structurally defined than molecular machines, making subtomogram identification and averaging more challenging. Key requirement: budding events must have sufficient structural features for particle picking. Curved membrane regions with protein enrichment can serve as fiducials.

Most cargo of interest (virulence factors, sRNA, enzymes) is luminal (inside the OMV). Cryo-EM/cryo-ET can visualize luminal contents as electron density but cannot identify specific proteins without labels or prior structural knowledge. Surface-displayed cargo (OMPs, lipoproteins) is more accessible to structural analysis. This limits the hypothesis space: cryo-EM is more informative for surface cargo sorting mechanisms than luminal cargo mechanisms.

checks.check: PubMed co-occurrence: cryo-EM AND outer membrane vesicle
checks.details: Estimated ~15-25 papers mention both cryo-EM and OMVs. Most are morphological studies. Zero papers apply heterogeneity analysis tools (cryoDRGN, 3DVA, 3D classification) to OMV populations. Confirms DISJOINT assessment for the analytical methodology transfer.

warnings: CryoDRGN designed for conformational heterogeneity of single complexes — may need adaptation for compositional heterogeneity of OMV populations. Generator should distinguish between conformational analysis of individual OMP complexes (cryoDRGN appropriate) vs population-level classification of OMVs (RELION 3D classification more appropriate).
Luminal cargo is not directly identifiable by cryo-EM without labels. Hypotheses should focus on surface cargo sorting mechanisms or budding site structural intermediates.
positive_signals: OMV size, yield, and purification are fully compatible with cryo-EM/cryo-ET requirements.
Subtomogram averaging of bacterial membrane processes is an established methodology.
The analytical gap (no heterogeneity analysis of OMVs) is confirmed — this is genuine undiscovered methodology transfer.
